Details: Removal of strychnine from water by membrane separation (specifically reverse osmosis) was very effective in four studies (98 percent removal [618] to greater than 99 percent removal [15, 16, 617]). The membrane units tested were components of four different point-of-use or point-of-entry drinking water treatment systems. The RO membrane of each system was tested alone and only for strychnine removal. Initial strychnine concentrations ranged between 900 and 1,100 micrograms per liter. One study showed that when permeate strychnine concentration exceeded 10 micrograms per liter, the use of a post-membrane carbon filter helped achieve greater than 99 percent removal of strychnine [618]. Contaminant removal by reverse osmosis is dependent upon membrane type, pH, recovery, and initial contaminant concentration.
